title: Red

infobox.value: is the [color](/wiki/Color "Color") at the long wavelength end of the [visible spectrum](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum") of light, next to [orange](/wiki/Orange_(colour) "Orange (colour)") and opposite [violet](/wiki/Violet_(color) "Violet (color)"). It has a [dominant wavelength](/wiki/Dominant_wavelength "Dominant wavelength") of approximately 625–750 [nanometers](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre").[[1]](#cite_note-Georgia_State_University_Department_of_Physics_and_Astronomy-1) It is a primary color in the [RGB color model](/wiki/RGB_color_model "RGB color model") and a secondary color (made from [magenta](/wiki/Magenta "Magenta") and [yellow](/wiki/Yellow "Yellow")) in the [CMYK color model](/wiki/CMYK_color_model "CMYK color model"), and is the [complementary color](/wiki/Complementary_color "Complementary color") of [cyan](/wiki/Cyan "Cyan"). Reds range from the brilliant yellow-tinged [scarlet](/wiki/Scarlet_(color) "Scarlet (color)") and [vermillion](/wiki/Vermilion "Vermilion") to bluish-red [crimson](/wiki/Crimson "Crimson"), and vary in shade from the pale red [pink](/wiki/Pink "Pink") to the dark red [burgundy](/wiki/Burgundy_(color) "Burgundy (color)").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Red** is the [color](/wiki/Color "Color") at the long wavelength end of the [visible spectrum](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum") of light, next to [orange](/wiki/Orange_(colour) "Orange (colour)") and opposite [violet](/wiki/Violet_(color) "Violet (color)"). It has a [dominant wavelength](/wiki/Dominant_wavelength "Dominant wavelength") of approximately 625–750 [nanometers](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre").[[1]](#cite_note-Georgia_State_University_Department_of_Physics_and_Astronomy-1) It is a primary color in the [RGB color model](/wiki/RGB_color_model "RGB color model") and a secondary color (made from [magenta](/wiki/Magenta "Magenta") and [yellow](/wiki/Yellow "Yellow")) in the [CMYK color model](/wiki/CMYK_color_model "CMYK color model"), and is the [complementary color](/wiki/Complementary_color "Complementary color") of [cyan](/wiki/Cyan "Cyan"). Reds range from the brilliant yellow-tinged [scarlet](/wiki/Scarlet_(color) "Scarlet (color)") and [vermillion](/wiki/Vermilion "Vermilion") to bluish-red [crimson](/wiki/Crimson "Crimson"), and vary in shade from the pale red [pink](/wiki/Pink "Pink") to the dark red [burgundy](/wiki/Burgundy_(color) "Burgundy (color)").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Red pigment made from [ochre](/wiki/Ochre "Ochre") was one of the first colors used in [prehistoric art](/wiki/Prehistoric_art "Prehistoric art"). The [Ancient Egyptians](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") and [Mayans](/wiki/Mayan_civilization "Mayan civilization") colored their faces red in ceremonies; [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") generals had their bodies colored red to celebrate victories. It was also an important color in [China](/wiki/China "China"), where it was used to color early pottery and later the gates and walls of palaces.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3): 60–61 In the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), the brilliant red costumes for the nobility and wealthy were dyed with [kermes](/wiki/Kermes_(dye) "Kermes (dye)") and [cochineal](/wiki/Cochineal "Cochineal"). The 19th century brought the introduction of the first synthetic red dyes, which replaced the traditional dyes. Red became [a symbolic color](/wiki/Red_(politics) "Red (politics)") of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism"); [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Soviet_Russia "Soviet Russia") adopted a [red flag](/wiki/Red_flag_(politics) "Red flag (politics)") following the [Bolshevik Revolution](/wiki/Bolshevik_Revolution "Bolshevik Revolution") in 1917. The [Soviet red banner](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Soviet_Union "Flag of the Soviet Union") would subsequently be used throughout the entire history of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). China adopted [its own red flag](/wiki/Flag_of_China "Flag of China") following the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution"). A red flag [was also adopted](/wiki/Flag_of_Vietnam "Flag of Vietnam") by [North Vietnam](/wiki/North_Vietnam "North Vietnam") in 1954, and by all of Vietnam in 1975.

Since red is the color of [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood"), it has historically been associated with sacrifice, danger, and courage. Modern surveys in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") show red is also the color most commonly associated with heat, activity, passion, sexuality, anger, love, and joy. In China, [India](/wiki/India "India"), and many other Asian countries it is the color symbolizing happiness and good fortune.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 39–63

## Shades and variations

Main article: [Shades of red](/wiki/Shades_of_red "Shades of red")

Varieties of the color red may differ in [hue](/wiki/Hue "Hue"), [chroma](/wiki/Colorfulness "Colorfulness") (also called saturation, intensity, or colorfulness), or [lightness](/wiki/Lightness "Lightness") (or value, tone, or [brightness](/wiki/Brightness "Brightness")), or in two or three of these qualities. Variations in value are also called [tints and shades](/wiki/Tints_and_shades "Tints and shades"), a tint being a red or other hue mixed with white, a shade being mixed with black. Four examples are shown below.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Cardinal.jpg/250px-Cardinal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cardinal.jpg)

The [cardinal](/wiki/Northern_cardinal "Northern cardinal") takes its name from the color worn by Catholic [cardinals](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Cherry_blossoms_in_the_Tsutsujigaoka_Park.jpg/330px-Cherry_blossoms_in_the_Tsutsujigaoka_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherry_blossoms_in_the_Tsutsujigaoka_Park.jpg)

[Pink](/wiki/Pink "Pink") is a pale shade of red. [Cherry blossoms](/wiki/Cherry_blossoms "Cherry blossoms") in the Tsutsujigaoka Park, [Sendai](/wiki/Sendai "Sendai"), [Miyagi](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture"), Japan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Red_tikka_powder.jpg/330px-Red_tikka_powder.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_tikka_powder.jpg)

[Vermilion](/wiki/Vermilion "Vermilion") is similar to scarlet, but slightly more orange. This is [sindoor](/wiki/Sindoor "Sindoor"), a red cosmetic powder used in India; some [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") women put a stripe of sindoor along their hair parting to show they are married.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Ruby_gem.JPG/330px-Ruby_gem.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ruby_gem.JPG)

[Ruby](/wiki/Ruby_(color) "Ruby (color)") is the color of a cut and polished [ruby](/wiki/Ruby "Ruby") gemstone.

## In science and nature

### Seeing red

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Madrid_Bullfight.JPG/250px-Madrid_Bullfight.JPG)](/wiki/File:Madrid_Bullfight.JPG)

Bulls, like dogs and many other animals, have [dichromacy](/wiki/Dichromacy "Dichromacy"), which means they cannot distinguish the color red. They charge the matador's cape because of its motion, not its color.

The human eye sees red when it looks at light with a wavelength between approximately 625 and 740 [nanometers](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre").[[1]](#cite_note-Georgia_State_University_Department_of_Physics_and_Astronomy-1) It is a primary color in the [RGB color model](/wiki/RGB_color_model "RGB color model") and the light just past this range is called infrared, or below red, and cannot be seen by human eyes, although it can be sensed as heat.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the language of optics, red is the color evoked by light that stimulates neither the S or the M (short and medium wavelength) cone cells of the retina, combined with a fading stimulation of the L (long-wavelength) cone cells.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Primates](/wiki/Primate "Primate") can distinguish the full range of the colors of the spectrum visible to humans, but many kinds of mammals, such as dogs and cattle, have [dichromacy](/wiki/Dichromacy "Dichromacy"), which means they can see blues and yellows, but cannot distinguish red and green (both are seen as gray). Bulls, for instance, cannot see the red color of the cape of a bullfighter, but they are agitated by its movement.[[9]](#cite_note-Ali&Klyne1985-9) (See [color vision](/wiki/Color_vision "Color vision")).

One theory for why primates developed sensitivity to red is that it allowed ripe fruit to be distinguished from unripe fruit and inedible vegetation.[[10]](#cite_note-10) This may have driven further adaptations by species taking advantage of this new ability, such as the emergence of red faces.[[11]](#cite_note-scidaily-11)

Red light is used to help adapt [night vision](/wiki/Adaptation_(eye) "Adaptation (eye)") in low-light or night time, as the [rod cells](/wiki/Rod_cell "Rod cell") in the human eye are not sensitive to red.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### In color theory and on a computer screen

In the [RYB color model](/wiki/RYB_color_model "RYB color model"), which is the basis of [traditional color theory](/wiki/Traditional_color_theory "Traditional color theory"), red is one of the three [primary colors](/wiki/Primary_color "Primary color"), along with blue and yellow. Painters in the Renaissance mixed red and blue to make violet: [Cennino Cennini](/wiki/Cennino_Cennini "Cennino Cennini"), in his 15th-century manual on painting, wrote, "If you want to make a lovely violet colour, take fine lac ([red lake](/wiki/Lake_pigments#History_and_art "Lake pigments")), [ultramarine blue](/wiki/Ultramarine_blue "Ultramarine blue") (the same amount of the one as of the other) with a binder"; he noted that it could also be made by mixing blue [indigo](/wiki/Indigo "Indigo") and red [hematite](/wiki/Hematite "Hematite").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In the CMY and [CMYK](/wiki/CMYK_color_model "CMYK color model") color models, red is a secondary color subtractively mixed from magenta and yellow.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the [RGB color model](/wiki/RGB_color_model "RGB color model"), red, green and blue are [additive primary colors](/wiki/Additive_primary_colors "Additive primary colors"). Red, green and blue light combined makes white light, and these three colors, combined in different mixtures, can produce nearly any other color. This principle is used to generate colors on such as computer monitors and televisions. For example, magenta on a computer screen is made by a similar formula to that used by Cennino Cennini in the Renaissance to make violet, but using [additive colors](/wiki/Additive_color "Additive color") and light instead of pigment: it is created by combining red and blue light at equal intensity on a black screen. Violet is made on a computer screen in a similar way, but with a greater amount of blue light and less red light.[[15]](#cite_note-huevaluechroma1-15)

### Color of sunset

Main article: [Sunset § Colors](/wiki/Sunset#Colors "Sunset")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Crimson_sunset.jpg/250px-Crimson_sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crimson_sunset.jpg)

Sunsets and sunrises are often red because of an optical effect called [Rayleigh scattering](/wiki/Rayleigh_scattering "Rayleigh scattering").

As a ray of white sunlight travels through the atmosphere to the eye, some of the colors are scattered out of the beam by air molecules and [airborne particles](/wiki/Atmospheric_particulate_matter "Atmospheric particulate matter") due to [Rayleigh scattering](/wiki/Rayleigh_scattering "Rayleigh scattering"), changing the final color of the beam that is seen. Colors with a shorter wavelength, such as blue and green, scatter more strongly, and are removed from the light that finally reaches the eye.[[16]](#cite_note-saha-16) At [sunrise](/wiki/Sunrise "Sunrise") and [sunset](/wiki/Sunset "Sunset"), when the path of the sunlight through the atmosphere to the eye is longest, the blue and green components are removed almost completely, leaving the longer wavelength orange and red light. The remaining reddened sunlight can also be scattered by cloud droplets and other relatively large particles, which give the sky above the horizon its red glow.[[17]](#cite_note-guenther-17)

### Lasers

[Lasers](/wiki/Laser "Laser") emitting in the red region of the spectrum have been available since the invention of the [ruby laser](/wiki/Ruby_laser "Ruby laser") in 1960. In 1962 the red [helium–neon laser](/wiki/Helium%E2%80%93neon_laser "Helium–neon laser") was invented,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and these two types of lasers were widely used in many scientific applications including [holography](/wiki/Holography "Holography"), and in education. Red helium–neon lasers were used commercially in [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc") players. The use of red [laser diodes](/wiki/Laser_diode "Laser diode") became widespread with the commercial success of modern [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") players, which use a 660 nm laser diode technology. Today, red and red-orange laser diodes are widely available to the public in the form of extremely inexpensive [laser pointers](/wiki/Laser_pointer "Laser pointer"). Portable, high-powered versions are also available for various applications.[[19]](#cite_note-laserglow-19) More recently, 671 nm diode-pumped solid state ([DPSS](/wiki/DPSS "DPSS")) lasers have been introduced to the market for all-DPSS laser display systems, [particle image velocimetry](/wiki/Particle_image_velocimetry "Particle image velocimetry"), [Raman spectroscopy](/wiki/Raman_spectroscopy "Raman spectroscopy"), and holography.[[20]](#cite_note-laserglow2-20)

Red's wavelength has been an important factor in laser technologies; red lasers, used in early [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") technologies, are being replaced by blue lasers, as red's longer wavelength causes the laser's recordings to take up more space on the disc than would blue-laser recordings.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Astronomy

* [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") is called the Red Planet because of the reddish color imparted to its surface by the abundant [iron oxide](/wiki/Iron_oxide "Iron oxide") present there.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* Astronomical objects that are moving away from the observer exhibit a Doppler [red shift](/wiki/Redshift "Redshift").
* [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter")'s surface displays a [Great Red Spot](/wiki/Great_Red_Spot "Great Red Spot") caused by an oval-shaped mega storm south of the planet's [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator").[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* [Red giants](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant") are stars that have exhausted the supply of [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") in their cores and switched to [thermonuclear fusion](/wiki/Thermonuclear_fusion "Thermonuclear fusion") of hydrogen in a shell that surrounds its core. They have radii tens to hundreds of times larger than that of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"). However, their outer envelope is much lower in temperature, giving them an orange hue. Despite the lower energy density of their envelope, red giants are many times more luminous than the Sun due to their large size.
* [Red supergiants](/wiki/Red_supergiant "Red supergiant") like [Betelgeuse](/wiki/Betelgeuse "Betelgeuse"), [Antares](/wiki/Antares "Antares"), [Mu Cephei](/wiki/Mu_Cephei "Mu Cephei"), [VV Cephei](/wiki/VV_Cephei "VV Cephei"), and [VY Canis Majoris](/wiki/VY_Canis_Majoris "VY Canis Majoris") one of the [biggest stars](/wiki/List_of_largest_known_stars "List of largest known stars") in the [Universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe"), are the biggest variety of red giants. They are huge in size, with radii 200 to 1700 times greater than the Sun, but relatively cool in temperature (3000–4500 K), causing their distinct red tint.
* A [red dwarf](/wiki/Red_dwarf "Red dwarf") is a small and relatively [cool](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature") [star](/wiki/Star "Star"), which has a mass of less than half that of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") and a surface temperature of less than 4,000 [K](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin"). Red dwarfs are by far the most common type of star in the Galaxy, but due to their low luminosity, from Earth, none are visible to the naked eye.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* [Interstellar reddening](/wiki/Interstellar_reddening "Interstellar reddening") is caused by the extinction of radiation by dust and gas

### Food coloring

The most common synthetic food coloring today is [Allura Red AC](/wiki/Allura_Red_AC "Allura Red AC"), a red [azo dye](/wiki/Azo_dye "Azo dye") that goes by several names including: **Allura Red**, **Food Red 17**, **C.I. 16035**, **FD&C Red 40**,[[25]](#cite_note-Shampoo-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Food_Colour_Facts-26) It was originally manufactured from coal tar, but now is mostly made from petroleum.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In Europe, Allura Red AC is not recommended for consumption by children. It is banned in Denmark, Belgium, France and Switzerland, and was also banned in Sweden until the country joined the European Union in 1994.[[26]](#cite_note-Food_Colour_Facts-26) The [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") approves Allura Red AC as a food colorant, but EU countries' local laws banning food colorants are preserved.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In the United States, Allura Red AC is approved by the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") (FDA) for use in [cosmetics](/wiki/Cosmetics "Cosmetics"), [drugs](/wiki/Drug "Drug"), and food. It is used in some tattoo inks and is used in many products, such as [soft drinks](/wiki/Soft_drink "Soft drink"), children's medications, and [cotton candy](/wiki/Cotton_candy "Cotton candy"). On June 30, 2010, the [Center for Science in the Public Interest](/wiki/Center_for_Science_in_the_Public_Interest "Center for Science in the Public Interest") (CSPI) called for the FDA to ban Red 40.[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Red 3](/wiki/Red_3 "Red 3") dye was banned in the United States in 2025.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Because of public concerns about possible health risks associated with synthetic dyes, many companies have switched to using natural pigments such as [carmine](/wiki/Carmine "Carmine"), made from crushing the tiny female [cochineal](/wiki/Cochineal "Cochineal") insect. This insect, originating in Mexico and Central America, was used to make the brilliant [scarlet](/wiki/Scarlet_(color) "Scarlet (color)") dyes of the European Renaissance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The red of autumn leaves is produced by pigments called [anthocyanins](/wiki/Anthocyanin "Anthocyanin"). They are not present in the leaf throughout the growing season, but are actively produced towards the end of summer.[[31]](#cite_note-tree1044-31) They develop in late summer in the [sap](/wiki/Sap "Sap") of the cells of the leaf, and this development is the result of complex interactions of many influences—both inside and outside the plant. Their formation depends on the breakdown of sugars in the presence of bright light as the level of [phosphate](/wiki/Phosphate "Phosphate") in the leaf is reduced.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

During the summer growing season, phosphate is at a high level. It has a vital role in the breakdown of the [sugars](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") manufactured by chlorophyll. But in the fall, phosphate, along with the other chemicals and nutrients, moves out of the leaf into the [stem](/wiki/Plant_stem "Plant stem") of the plant. When this happens, the sugar-breakdown process changes, leading to the production of anthocyanin pigments. The brighter the light during this period, the greater the production of anthocyanins and the more brilliant the resulting color display. When the days of autumn are bright and cool, and the nights are chilly but not freezing, the brightest colorations usually develop.

Anthocyanins temporarily color the edges of some of the very young [leaves](/wiki/Leaf "Leaf") as they unfold from the [buds](/wiki/Bud "Bud") in early spring. They also give the familiar color to such common fruits as [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberry "Cranberry"), [red apples](/wiki/Red_Delicious "Red Delicious"), [blueberries](/wiki/Blueberry "Blueberry"), [cherries](/wiki/Cherry "Cherry"), [raspberries](/wiki/Raspberry "Raspberry"), and [plums](/wiki/Plum "Plum").

Anthocyanins are present in about 10% of tree species in temperate regions, although in certain areas—a [famous example being New England](/wiki/Autumn_in_New_England "Autumn in New England")—up to 70% of tree species may produce the pigment.[[31]](#cite_note-tree1044-31) In autumn forests they appear vivid in the [maples](/wiki/Maple "Maple"), [oaks](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [sourwood](/wiki/Sourwood "Sourwood"), [sweetgums](/wiki/Liquidambar "Liquidambar"), [dogwoods](/wiki/Dogwood "Dogwood"), [tupelos](/wiki/Tupelo_(tree) "Tupelo (tree)"), [cherry](/wiki/Cherry "Cherry") trees and [persimmons](/wiki/Persimmon "Persimmon"). These same pigments often combine with the carotenoids' colors to create the deeper orange, fiery reds, and bronzes typical of many hardwood species. (See [Autumn leaf color](/wiki/Autumn_leaf_color "Autumn leaf color")).

Oxygenated blood is red due to the presence of oxygenated [hemoglobin](/wiki/Hemoglobin "Hemoglobin") that contains iron molecules, with the iron components reflecting red light.[[33]](#cite_note-UCSBblood-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Red meat gets its color from the iron found in the [myoglobin](/wiki/Myoglobin "Myoglobin") and hemoglobin in the muscles and residual blood.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Plants like [apples](/wiki/Apple "Apple"), [strawberries](/wiki/Garden_strawberry "Garden strawberry"), [cherries](/wiki/Cherry "Cherry"), [tomatoes](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), [peppers](/wiki/Capsicum "Capsicum"), and [pomegranates](/wiki/Pomegranate "Pomegranate") are often colored by forms of [carotenoids](/wiki/Carotenoid "Carotenoid"), red pigments that also assist [photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Hair color

Main article: [Red hair](/wiki/Red_hair "Red hair")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Woman_redhead_natural_portrait_1.jpg/250px-Woman_redhead_natural_portrait_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Woman_redhead_natural_portrait_1.jpg)

Red hair only occurs in 1–2% of the human population.

Red hair occurs naturally on approximately 1–2% of the human population.[[37]](#cite_note-37) It occurs more frequently (2–6%) in people of northern or western European ancestry, and less frequently in other populations. Red hair appears in people with two copies of a [recessive gene](/wiki/Dominance_relationship "Dominance relationship") on [chromosome 16](/wiki/Chromosome_16 "Chromosome 16") which causes a mutation in the [MC1R](/wiki/Melanocortin_1_receptor "Melanocortin 1 receptor") protein.[[38]](#cite_note-thetech-38)

Red hair varies from a deep [burgundy](/wiki/Burgundy_(color) "Burgundy (color)") through [burnt orange](/wiki/Orange_(color)#Burnt_orange "Orange (color)") to bright [copper](/wiki/Copper_(color) "Copper (color)"). It is characterized by high levels of the reddish pigment [pheomelanin](/wiki/Pheomelanin "Pheomelanin") (which also accounts for the red color of the lips) and relatively low levels of the dark pigment [eumelanin](/wiki/Eumelanin "Eumelanin"). The term "redhead" (originally *redd hede*) has been in use since at least 1510.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### In animal and human behavior

Red is associated with [dominance](/wiki/Dominance_hierarchy "Dominance hierarchy") in a number of animal species.[[40]](#cite_note-Little_&_Hill_2007-40) For example, in [mandrills](/wiki/Mandrill "Mandrill"), red coloration of the face is greatest in [alpha](/wiki/Dominance_hierarchy "Dominance hierarchy") males, increasingly less prominent in lower ranking subordinates, and directly correlated with levels of [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone "Testosterone").[[41]](#cite_note-41) Red can also affect the perception of dominance by others, leading to significant differences in mortality, [reproductive success](/wiki/Reproductive_success "Reproductive success") and [parental investment](/wiki/Parental_investment "Parental investment") between individuals displaying red and those not.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In humans, wearing red has been linked with increased performance in competitions, including professional sport[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) and [multiplayer video games](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Controlled tests have demonstrated that wearing red does not increase performance or levels of testosterone during exercise, so the effect is likely to be produced by perceived rather than actual performance.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Judges of [tae kwon do](/wiki/Tae_kwon_do "Tae kwon do") have been shown to favor competitors wearing red protective gear over blue,[[47]](#cite_note-47) and, when asked, a significant majority of people say that red abstract shapes are more "dominant", "aggressive", and "likely to win a physical competition" than blue shapes.[[40]](#cite_note-Little_&_Hill_2007-40) In contrast to its positive effect in physical competition and dominance behavior, exposure to red decreases performance in cognitive tasks[[48]](#cite_note-48) and elicits aversion in psychological tests where subjects are placed in an "achievement" context (e.g. taking an [IQ test](/wiki/IQ_test "IQ test")).[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## History and art

See also: [History of red](/wiki/History_of_red "History of red")

### In prehistory and the ancient world

  Roman wall painting showing a dye shop, Pompeii (40 BC)

Inside cave 13B at [Pinnacle Point](/wiki/Pinnacle_Point "Pinnacle Point"), an archeological site found on the coast of South Africa, [paleoanthropologists](/wiki/Paleoanthropologists "Paleoanthropologists") in 2000 found evidence that, between 170,000 and 40,000 years ago, [Late Stone Age](/wiki/Late_Stone_Age "Late Stone Age") people were scraping and grinding [ochre](/wiki/Ochre "Ochre"), a clay colored red by [iron oxide](/wiki/Iron_oxide "Iron oxide"), probably with the intention of using it to color their bodies.[[50]](#cite_note-Marean_2007-50)

Red [hematite](/wiki/Hematite "Hematite") powder was also found scattered around the remains at a grave site in a [Zhoukoudian](/wiki/Zhoukoudian "Zhoukoudian") cave complex near [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"). The site has evidence of habitation as early as 700,000 years ago. The hematite might have been used to symbolize blood in an offering to the dead.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3): 4

Red, black and white were the first colors used by artists in the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") age, probably because natural pigments such as red ochre and iron oxide were readily available where early people lived. [Madder](/wiki/Rubia "Rubia"), a plant whose root could be made into a red dye, grew widely in Europe, Africa and Asia.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The [cave of Altamira](/wiki/Cave_of_Altamira "Cave of Altamira") in Spain has a painting of a bison colored with red ochre that dates to between 15,000 and 16,500 BC.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

A red dye called [Kermes](/wiki/Kermes_(dye) "Kermes (dye)") was made beginning in the [Neolithic Period](/wiki/Neolithic_Period "Neolithic Period") by drying and then crushing the bodies of the females of a tiny [scale insect](/wiki/Scale_insect "Scale insect") in the genus *[Kermes](/wiki/Kermes_(insect) "Kermes (insect)")*, primarily *[Kermes vermilio](/wiki/Kermes_vermilio "Kermes vermilio")*. The insects live on the sap of certain trees, especially [Kermes oak](/wiki/Kermes_oak "Kermes oak") trees near the Mediterranean region. Jars of kermes have been found in a Neolithic cave-burial at Adaoutse, [Bouches-du-Rhône](/wiki/Bouches-du-Rh%C3%B4ne "Bouches-du-Rhône").[[53]](#cite_note-53): 230–31 Kermes from oak trees was later used by Romans, who imported it from Spain. A different variety of dye was made from [*Porphyrophora hamelii* (Armenian cochineal)](/wiki/Armenian_cochineal "Armenian cochineal") scale insects that lived on the roots and stems of certain herbs. It was mentioned in texts as early as the 8th century BC, and it was used by the ancient Assyrians and Persians.[[54]](#cite_note-:3-54): 45

In ancient Egypt, red was associated with life, health, and victory. Egyptians would color themselves with red ochre during celebrations.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Egyptian women used red ochre as a [cosmetic](/wiki/Cosmetics "Cosmetics") to redden cheeks and lips[[56]](#cite_note-56) and also used [henna](/wiki/Henna "Henna") to color their hair and paint their nails.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

The ancient Romans wore [togas](/wiki/Togas "Togas") with red stripes on holidays, and the bride at a wedding wore a red shawl, called a *flammeum*.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 46 Red was used to color statues and the skin of gladiators. Red was also the color associated with army; Roman soldiers wore red tunics, and officers wore a cloak called a [paludamentum](/wiki/Paludamentum "Paludamentum") which, depending upon the quality of the dye, could be crimson, [scarlet](/wiki/Scarlet_(color) "Scarlet (color)") or purple. In [Roman mythology](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology") red is associated with the god of war, [Mars](/wiki/Mars_(god) "Mars (god)").[[58]](#cite_note-:2-58) The [vexilloid](/wiki/Vexilloid "Vexilloid") of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") had a red background with the letters [SPQR](/wiki/SPQR "SPQR") in [gold](/wiki/Gold_(color) "Gold (color)"). A Roman general receiving a [triumph](/wiki/Roman_triumph "Roman triumph") had his entire body painted red in honor of his achievement.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The Romans liked bright colors, and many [Roman villas](/wiki/Roman_villas "Roman villas") were decorated with vivid red murals. The pigment used for many of the murals was called [vermilion](/wiki/Vermilion "Vermilion"), and it came from the mineral [cinnabar](/wiki/Cinnabar "Cinnabar"), a common ore of [mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(element) "Mercury (element)"). It was one of the finest reds of ancient times – the paintings have retained their brightness for more than twenty centuries. The source of cinnabar for the Romans was a group of mines near [Almadén](/wiki/Almad%C3%A9n "Almadén"), southwest of [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), in Spain. Working in the mines was extremely dangerous, since mercury is highly toxic; the miners were slaves or prisoners, and being sent to the cinnabar mines was a virtual death sentence.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

  The color of majesty - portrait of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), King of the Franks and Holy Roman Emperor, Netherlands (14th c.)

After the fall of the Western Roman Empire, red was adopted as a color of majesty and authority by the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), and the princes of Europe. It also played an important part in the rituals of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"), symbolizing the blood of Christ and the Christian martyrs.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In Western Europe, Emperor [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") painted his palace red as a very visible symbol of his authority, and wore red shoes at his coronation.[[54]](#cite_note-:3-54): 36–37 Kings, princes and, beginning in 1295, Roman Catholic cardinals began to wear red colored [habitus](/wiki/Religious_habit "Religious habit"). When [Abbe Suger](/wiki/Abbe_Suger "Abbe Suger") rebuilt [Saint Denis Basilica](/wiki/Saint_Denis_Basilica "Saint Denis Basilica") outside Paris in the early 12th century, he added [stained glass](/wiki/Stained_glass "Stained glass") windows colored blue cobalt glass and red glass tinted with copper. Together they flooded the basilica with a mystical light. Soon stained glass windows were being added to cathedrals all across France, England and Germany. In medieval painting red was used to attract attention to the most important figures; both [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ") and the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") were commonly painted wearing red mantles.

In western countries red is a symbol of martyrs and sacrifice, particularly because of its association with blood.[[58]](#cite_note-:2-58) Beginning in the Middle Ages, the Pope and Cardinals of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") wore red to symbolize the blood of Christ and the Christian martyrs. The banner of the Christian soldiers in the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") was a red cross on a white field, the [St. George's Cross](/wiki/St._George%27s_Cross "St. George's Cross"). According to Christian tradition, [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") was a Roman soldier who was a member of the guards of the Emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian"), who refused to renounce his Christian faith and was martyred. The Saint George's Cross became the [Flag of England](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England") in the 16th century, and now is part of the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") of the United Kingdom, as well as the Flag of the Republic of [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)").[[54]](#cite_note-:3-54): 36

In [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") painting, red was used to draw the attention of the viewer; it was often used as the color of the cloak or costume of [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ"), the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary"), or another central figure.

In [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), [Titian](/wiki/Titian "Titian") was the master of fine reds, particularly [vermilion](/wiki/Vermilion "Vermilion"); he used many layers of pigment mixed with a semi-transparent glaze, which let the light pass through, to create a more luminous color. The figures of God, the Virgin Mary and two apostles are highlighted by their vermilion red costumes.

Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") of England liked to wear bright reds, before she adopted the more sober image of the "Virgin Queen".

Red costumes were not limited to the upper classes. In Renaissance [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), people of all social classes wore red at celebrations. One such celebration was captured in *[The Wedding Dance](/wiki/The_Wedding_Dance "The Wedding Dance")* (1566) by [Pieter Bruegel the Elder](/wiki/Pieter_Bruegel_the_Elder "Pieter Bruegel the Elder").

The painter [Johannes Vermeer](/wiki/Johannes_Vermeer "Johannes Vermeer") skilfully used different shades and tints of vermilion to paint the red skirt in *[The Girl with the Wine Glass](/wiki/The_Girl_with_the_Wine_Glass "The Girl with the Wine Glass")*, then glazed it with madder lake to make a more luminous color.

In the 18th century, red began to take on a new identity as the color of resistance and revolution. It was already associated with blood, and with danger; a red flag hoisted before a battle meant that no prisoners would be taken. In 1793–94, red became the color of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). A red [Phrygian cap](/wiki/Phrygian_cap "Phrygian cap"), or "liberty cap", was part of the uniform of the [sans-culottes](/wiki/Sans-culottes "Sans-culottes"), the most militant faction of the revolutionaries.[[63]](#cite_note-auto-63)

In the late 18th century, during a strike English dock workers carried red flags, and it thereafter became closely associated with the new labor movement, and later with the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") in the United Kingdom, founded in 1900.

In Paris in 1832, a red flag was carried by working-class demonstrators in the failed [June Rebellion](/wiki/June_Rebellion "June Rebellion") (an event immortalized in *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables "Les Misérables")*), and later in the [1848 French Revolution](/wiki/1848_French_Revolution "1848 French Revolution").[[64]](#cite_note-marh-64) The red flag was proposed as the new national French flag during the 1848 revolution, but was rejected by at the urging of the poet and statesman [Alphonse Lamartine](/wiki/Alphonse_Lamartine "Alphonse Lamartine") in favor of the tricolor flag. It appeared again as the flag of the short-lived [Paris Commune](/wiki/Paris_Commune "Paris Commune") in 1871. It was then adopted by [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and the new European movements of [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") and [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"). [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") adopted a red flag following the [Bolshevik Revolution](/wiki/Bolshevik_Revolution "Bolshevik Revolution") in 1917. The People's Republic of China adopted the red flag following the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution"). It was adopted by [North Vietnam](/wiki/North_Vietnam "North Vietnam") in 1954, and by all of Vietnam in 1975.

## Symbolism

### Courage and sacrifice

Surveys show that red is the color most associated with courage.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 43 In western countries red is a symbol of martyrs and sacrifice, particularly because of its association with blood.[[58]](#cite_note-:2-58) Beginning in the Middle Ages, the Pope and Cardinals of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") wore red to symbolize the blood of Christ and the Christian martyrs. The banner of the Christian soldiers in the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") was a red cross on a white field, the [St. George's Cross](/wiki/St._George%27s_Cross "St. George's Cross"). According to Christian tradition, [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") was a Roman soldier who was a member of the guards of the Emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian"), who refused to renounce his Christian faith and was martyred. The Saint George's Cross became the [Flag of England](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England") in the 16th century, and now is part of the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") of the United Kingdom, as well as the Flag of the Republic of [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)").[[54]](#cite_note-:3-54): 36

### Hatred, anger, aggression, passion, heat and war

While red is the color most associated with love, it also the color most frequently associated with [hatred](/wiki/Hatred "Hatred"), [anger](/wiki/Anger "Anger"), [aggression](/wiki/Aggression "Aggression") and war. People who are angry are said to "*[see red](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/see_red#English "wikt:see red")*." Red is the color most commonly associated with passion and heat. In [ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), red was the color of [Mars](/wiki/Mars_(mythology) "Mars (mythology)"), the [god of war](/wiki/God_of_war "God of war")—the planet [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") was named for him because of its red color.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 42, 53

### Warning and danger

Red is the traditional color of warning and danger, and is therefore often used on flags. In the Middle Ages up through the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), a [red flag](/wiki/Red_flag_(signal) "Red flag (signal)") shown in warfare indicated the intent to take no prisoners.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) Similarly, a red flag hoisted by a [pirate ship](/wiki/Pirate_ship "Pirate ship") meant no mercy would be shown to their target.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) In Britain, in the early days of motoring, motor cars had to follow a man with a red flag who would warn horse-drawn vehicles, before the [Locomotives on Highways Act 1896](/wiki/Locomotives_on_Highways_Act_1896 "Locomotives on Highways Act 1896") abolished this law.[[69]](#cite_note-69) In automobile races, the red flag is raised if there is danger to the drivers.[[70]](#cite_note-70) In international football, a player who has made a serious violation of the rules is shown a red [penalty card](/wiki/Penalty_card "Penalty card") and ejected from the game.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Several studies have indicated that red carries the strongest reaction of all the colors, with the level of reaction decreasing gradually with the colors orange, yellow, and white, respectively.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) For this reason, red is generally used as the highest level of warning, such as threat level of terrorist attack in the United States. In fact, teachers at a primary school in the UK have been told not to mark children's work in [red ink](/wiki/Red_ink "Red ink") because it encourages a "negative approach".[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Red is the international color of stop signs and stop lights on highways and intersections. It was standardized as the international color at the [Vienna Convention on Road Signs and Signals](/wiki/Vienna_Convention_on_Road_Signs_and_Signals "Vienna Convention on Road Signs and Signals") of 1968. It was chosen partly because red is the brightest color in daytime (next to orange), though it is less visible at twilight, when green is the most visible color. Red also stands out more clearly against a cool natural backdrop of blue sky, green trees or gray buildings. But it was mostly chosen as the color for stoplights and stop signs because of its universal association with danger and warning.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 54 The 1968 [Vienna Convention on Road Signs and Signals](/wiki/Vienna_Convention_on_Road_Signs_and_Signals "Vienna Convention on Road Signs and Signals") of 1968 uses red color also for the margin of danger warning sign, give way signs and prohibitory signs, following the previous German-type signage (established by Verordnung über Warnungstafeln für den Kraftfahrzeugverkehr in 1927).

### The color that attracts attention

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Magdalena_Frackowiak.jpg/250px-Magdalena_Frackowiak.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magdalena_Frackowiak.jpg)

Fashion model [Magdalena Frackowiak](/wiki/Magdalena_Frackowiak "Magdalena Frackowiak") at [Paris Fashion Week](/wiki/Paris_Fashion_Week "Paris Fashion Week") (Fall 2011)

Red is the color that most attracts attention. Surveys show it is the color most frequently associated with visibility, proximity, and extroverts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is also the color most associated with dynamism and activity.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 48, 58

Red is used in modern fashion much as it was used in Medieval painting; to attract the eyes of the viewer to the person who is supposed to be the center of attention. People wearing red seem to be closer than those dressed in other colors, even if they are actually the same distance away.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 48, 58 Monarchs, wives of presidential candidates and other celebrities often wear red to be visible from a distance in a crowd. It is also commonly worn by lifeguards and others whose job requires them to be easily found.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Because red attracts attention, it is frequently used in advertising, though studies show that people are less likely to read something printed in red because they know it is advertising, and because it is more difficult visually to read than black and white text.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 60

### Seduction, sexuality and sin

Red by a large margin is the color most commonly associated with seduction, sexuality, eroticism and immorality, possibly because of its close connection with passion and with danger.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4): 55

Red was long seen as having a dark side, particularly in [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") theology. It was associated with sexual passion, anger, sin, and the devil.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-StClair-78) In the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), the [Book of Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah") said: "Though your sins be as scarlet, they shall be white as snow."[[79]](#cite_note-79) In the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), in the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation"), the Antichrist appears as a red monster, ridden by a woman dressed in scarlet, known as the [Whore of Babylon](/wiki/Whore_of_Babylon "Whore of Babylon").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

[Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan") is often depicted as colored red and/or wearing a red costume in both iconography and [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture").[[78]](#cite_note-StClair-78)[[81]](#cite_note-81) By the 20th century, the devil in red had become a folk character in legends and stories. The devil in red appears more often in cartoons and movies than in religious art.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 17th-century New England, red was associated with [adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery"). In the 1850 novel by [Nathaniel Hawthorne](/wiki/Nathaniel_Hawthorne "Nathaniel Hawthorne"), *[The Scarlet Letter](/wiki/The_Scarlet_Letter "The Scarlet Letter")*, set in a [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") community, a woman is punished for adultery with ostracism, her sin represented by a red letter 'A' sewn onto her clothes.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[78]](#cite_note-StClair-78)

Red is still commonly associated with [prostitution](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution"). At various points in history, prostitutes were required to wear red to announce their profession.[[78]](#cite_note-StClair-78) Houses of prostitution displayed a red light. Beginning in the early 20th century, houses of prostitution were allowed only in certain specified neighborhoods, which became known as [red-light districts](/wiki/Red-light_districts "Red-light districts"). Large red-light districts are found today in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") and [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam").[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

In the [handkerchief code](/wiki/Handkerchief_code "Handkerchief code"), the color red signifies interest in the sexual act of [fisting](/wiki/Fisting "Fisting").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

In both Christian and Hebrew tradition, red is also sometimes associated with murder or guilt, with "having blood on one's hands", or "being caught red-handed.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

## In religion

* In [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), red is associated with the [blood of Christ](/wiki/Blood_of_Christ "Blood of Christ") and the sacrifice of [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr"). In the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") it is also associated with [pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") and the Holy Spirit. Since 1295, it is the color worn by Cardinals, the senior clergy of the Roman Catholic Church. Red is the [liturgical color](/wiki/Liturgical_color "Liturgical color") for the feasts of martyrs, representing the blood of those who suffered death for their faith. It is sometimes used as the liturgical color for [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), including [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") and [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), although this is a modern (20th-century) development. In Catholic practice, it is also the liturgical color used to commemorate the Holy Spirit (for this reason it is worn at Pentecost and during Confirmation masses). Because of its association with martyrdom and the Spirit, it is also the color used to commemorate saints who were martyred, such as St. George and all the Apostles (except for the Apostle St. John, who was not martyred, where white is used). As such, it is used to commemorate bishops, who are the successors of the Apostles (for this reason, when funeral masses are held for bishops, cardinals, or popes, red is used instead of the white that would ordinarily be used).
* In [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), red is one of the five colors which are said to have emanated from the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha") when he attained enlightenment, or [nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana "Nirvana"). It is particularly associated with the benefits of the practice of Buddhism; achievement, wisdom, virtue, fortune and dignity. It was also believed to have the power to resist evil. In China red was commonly used for the walls, pillars, and gates of temples.
* In the [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") religion of Japan, the gateways of temples, called [torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii"), are traditionally painted vermilion red and black. The torii symbolizes the passage from the profane world to a sacred place. The bridges in the gardens of Japanese temples are also painted red (and usually only temple bridges are red, not bridges in ordinary gardens), since they are also passages to sacred places. Red was also considered a color which could expel evil and disease.
* In [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), red is sometimes used to symbolize [yang](/wiki/Yin_and_yang "Yin and yang").[[87]](#cite_note-87)
* In [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"), red is also sometimes used to symbolize yang in the context of the creator [Pangu](/wiki/Pangu "Pangu"), who hatched out of a [cosmic egg](/wiki/World_egg "World egg") colored like a *[taijitu](/wiki/Taijitu "Taijitu")*.[[88]](#cite_note-:5-88) Some art of Pangu colored yang as red.[[88]](#cite_note-:5-88) In addition, red is also an [auspicious](/wiki/Luck "Luck") color according to Chinese beliefs.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

The red military uniform was adopted by the English Parliament's [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army") in 1645, and was still worn as a dress uniform by the British Army until the outbreak of the First World War in August 1914. Ordinary soldiers wore red coats dyed with [madder](/wiki/Rubia "Rubia"), while officers wore scarlet coats dyed with the more expensive [cochineal](/wiki/Cochineal "Cochineal").[[54]](#cite_note-:3-54): 168–69 This led to British soldiers' being known as [red coats](/wiki/Red_coat_(British_army) "Red coat (British army)").

In the modern British army, scarlet is still worn by the [Foot Guards](/wiki/Foot_Guards#British_Army "Foot Guards"), the [Life Guards](/wiki/Life_Guards_(British_Army) "Life Guards (British Army)"), and by some regimental bands or [drummers](/wiki/Drum "Drum") for ceremonial purposes. [Officers](/wiki/Commissioned_Officer "Commissioned Officer") and [NCOs](/wiki/Non-Commissioned_Officer "Non-Commissioned Officer") of those regiments which previously wore red retain scarlet as the color of their "mess" or formal evening jackets. The [Royal Gibraltar Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Gibraltar_Regiment "Royal Gibraltar Regiment") has a scarlet tunic in its winter dress.

Scarlet is worn for some full dress, military band or mess uniforms in the modern armies of a number of the countries that made up the former British Empire. These include the Australian, Jamaican, New Zealand, Fijian, Canadian, Kenyan, Ghanaian, Indian, Singaporean, Sri Lankan and Pakistani armies.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

The musicians of the [United States Marine Corps Band](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps_Band "United States Marine Corps Band") wear red, following an 18th-century military tradition that the uniforms of band members are the reverse of the uniforms of the other soldiers in their unit. Since the US Marine uniform is blue with red facings, the band wears the reverse.

[Red Serge](/wiki/Red_Serge "Red Serge") is the uniform of the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police"), created in 1873 as the [North-West Mounted Police](/wiki/North-West_Mounted_Police "North-West Mounted Police"), and given its present name in 1920. The uniform was adapted from the tunic of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"). Cadets at the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada") also wear red dress uniforms.

The [Brazilian Marine Corps](/wiki/Brazilian_Marine_Corps "Brazilian Marine Corps") wears a red dress uniform.

The first known team sport to feature red uniforms was [chariot racing](/wiki/Chariot_racing "Chariot racing") during the late [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). The earliest races were between two chariots, one driver wearing red, the other white. Later, the number of teams was increased to four, including drivers in light green and sky blue. Twenty-five races were run in a day, with a total of one hundred chariots participating.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Today many sports teams throughout the world feature red on their uniforms. Along with [blue](/wiki/Blue "Blue"), red is the most commonly used non-white color in sports. Numerous national sports teams wear red, often through association with their national flags. A few of these teams feature the color as part of their nickname such as Spain (with their [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") (soccer) national team nicknamed *La Furia Roja* or "The Red Fury") and Belgium (whose [football team](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") bears the nickname *Rode Duivels* or "Red Devils").

In club [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") (soccer), red is a commonly used color throughout the world. Among European notable club teams most often playing at home in red shirts include [Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich"), [Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica"), [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."), [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") and [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma"). Furthermore, many prominent teams play in partially red color schemes, involving different-colored sleeves or stripes. A number of teams' nicknames feature the color. A red [penalty card](/wiki/Penalty_card "Penalty card") is issued to a player who commits a serious infraction: the player is immediately disqualified from further play and his team must continue with one fewer player for the game's duration.

[Rosso Corsa](/wiki/Rosso_Corsa "Rosso Corsa") is the red international motor racing color of cars entered by teams from Italy. Since the 1920s Italian race cars of [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo "Alfa Romeo"), [Maserati](/wiki/Maserati "Maserati"), [Lancia](/wiki/Lancia "Lancia"), and later [Ferrari](/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari") and [Abarth](/wiki/Abarth "Abarth") have been painted with a color known as *rosso corsa* ("racing red"). National colors were mostly replaced in [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") by commercial sponsor liveries in 1968, but unlike most other teams, Ferrari always kept the traditional red, although the shade of the color varies. [Ducati](/wiki/Ducati_Corse "Ducati Corse") traditionally run red factory bikes in motorcycle [World Championship](/wiki/MotoGP "MotoGP") racing.

The color is commonly used for professional sports teams in Canada and the United States with eleven [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") teams, eleven [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") teams, seven [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") teams and eleven [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") teams prominently featuring some shade of the color. The color is also featured in the league logos of Major League Baseball, the National Football League and the National Basketball Association.[[93]](#cite_note-93) In the National Football League, a red flag is thrown by the head coach to challenge a referee's decision during the game. During the 1950s when red was strongly associated with [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") in the United States, the modern [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") team was known as the "Redlegs" and the term was used on baseball cards. After the red scare faded, the team was known as the "Reds" again.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

In [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), red is often the color used on a fighter's gloves. [George Foreman](/wiki/George_Foreman "George Foreman") wore the same red trunks he used during his loss to [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali") when he defeated [Michael Moorer](/wiki/Michael_Moorer "Michael Moorer") 20 years later to regain the title he lost. Boxers named or nicknamed "red" include [Red Burman](/wiki/Red_Burman "Red Burman"), [Ernie "Red" Lopez](/wiki/Ernie_Lopez "Ernie Lopez"), and his brother [Danny "Little Red" Lopez](/wiki/Danny_Lopez_(boxer) "Danny Lopez (boxer)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Countries_with_red_on_their_flags.svg/250px-Countries_with_red_on_their_flags.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Countries_with_red_on_their_flags.svg)

Countries with red on their flags; the shades of red correspond to those on their respective flags.

Red is the most common color found in national flags, found on the flags of 77 percent of the 210 countries listed as independent in 2016; far ahead of white (58 percent); green (40 percent) and blue (37 percent).[[95]](#cite_note-95) The [British flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") bears the colors red, white and blue; it includes the [cross](/wiki/Cross_(heraldry) "Cross (heraldry)") of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George"), patron saint of England, and the [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") of [Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Flag "Saint Patrick's Flag"), patron saint of Ireland, both of which are red on white.[[96]](#cite_note-:8-96): 10 The [flag of the United States](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_States "Flag of the United States") bears the colors of Britain,[[97]](#cite_note-97) the colors of the French *[tricolore](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France")* include red as part of the old Paris coat of arms, and other countries' flags, such as those of [Australia](/wiki/Flag_of_Australia "Flag of Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/Flag_of_New_Zealand "Flag of New Zealand"), and [Fiji](/wiki/Flag_of_Fiji "Flag of Fiji"), carry a small inset of the British flag in memory of their ties to that country.[[96]](#cite_note-:8-96): 13–20 Many former colonies of Spain, such as [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), also feature red-one of the colors of the Spanish flag-on their own banners. Red flags are also used to symbolize storms, bad water conditions, and many other dangers.

The red on the [flag of Nepal](/wiki/Flag_of_Nepal "Flag of Nepal") represents the [floral emblem](/wiki/Floral_emblem "Floral emblem") of the country, the [rhododendron](/wiki/Rhododendron "Rhododendron").

Red, blue, and white are also the [Pan-Slavic colors](/wiki/Pan-Slavic_colors "Pan-Slavic colors") adopted by the Slavic solidarity movement of the late nineteenth century. Initially these were the colors of the Russian flag; as the Slavic movement grew, they were adopted by other Slavic peoples including [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks"), [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes"), and [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs"). The flags of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Czech_Republic "Flag of the Czech Republic") and [Poland](/wiki/Flag_of_Poland "Flag of Poland") use red for historic heraldic reasons (see [Coat of arms of Poland](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Poland "Coat of arms of Poland") and [Coat of arms of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_the_Czech_Republic "Coat of arms of the Czech Republic")) & not due to Pan-Slavic connotations. In 2004 [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") adopted a new white [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Georgia_(country) "Flag of Georgia (country)"), which consists of four small and one big red cross in the middle touching all four sides.

Red, white, and black were the colors of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") from 1870 to 1918, and as such they came to be associated with German nationalism. In the 1920s they were adopted as the colors of the [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") flag. In *[Mein Kampf](/wiki/Mein_Kampf "Mein Kampf")*, Hitler explained that they were "revered colors expressive of our homage to the glorious past." The red part of the flag was also chosen to attract attention – Hitler wrote: "the new flag ... should prove effective as a large poster" because "in hundreds of thousands of cases a really striking emblem may be the first cause of awakening interest in a movement." The red also symbolized the social program of the Nazis, aimed at German workers.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Several designs by a number of different authors were considered, but the one adopted in the end was Hitler's personal design.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Red, white, green and black are the colors of [Pan-Arabism](/wiki/Pan-Arabism "Pan-Arabism") and are used by many Arab countries.[[100]](#cite_note-flags-100)

Red, gold, green, and black are the colors of [Pan-Africanism](/wiki/Pan-Africanism "Pan-Africanism"). Several African countries thus use the color on their flags, including South Africa, [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal"), [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo"), [Guinea](/wiki/Guinea "Guinea"), [Benin](/wiki/Benin "Benin"), and [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"). The [Pan-African colors](/wiki/Pan-African_colours "Pan-African colours") are borrowed from the [flag of Ethiopia](/wiki/Flag_of_Ethiopia "Flag of Ethiopia"), one of the oldest independent African countries.[[100]](#cite_note-flags-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101) Rwanda, notably, removed red from [its flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Rwanda "Flag of Rwanda") after the [Rwandan genocide](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide") because of red's association with blood.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

The flags of Japan and [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") both have a red circle in the middle of different colored backgrounds. The flag of the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") has a red trapezoid on the bottom signifying blood, courage, and valor (also, if the flag is inverted so that the red trapezoid is on top and the blue at the bottom, it indicates a state of war). The flag of [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") has a red rectangle on the top. The field of the [flag of Portugal](/wiki/Flag_of_Portugal "Flag of Portugal") is green and red. The Ottoman Empire adopted several different red flags during the six centuries of its rule, with the successor [Republic of Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") continuing the 1844 Ottoman [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Turkey "Flag of Turkey").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Sozialdemokratische_Partei_Deutschlands%2C_Logo_um_2000.svg/250px-Sozialdemokratische_Partei_Deutschlands%2C_Logo_um_2000.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Sozialdemokratische_Partei_Deutschlands,_Logo_um_2000.svg)

In 18th-century Europe, red was usually associated with the monarchy and with those in power. The [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") wore red, as did the [Swiss Guards](/wiki/Swiss_Guards "Swiss Guards") of the [Kings of France](/wiki/Kings_of_France "Kings of France"), the soldiers of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") and the [Danish Army](/wiki/Danish_Army "Danish Army").

In the Roman Empire, freed slaves were given a red [Phrygian cap](/wiki/Phrygian_cap "Phrygian cap") as an emblem of their liberation. Because of this symbolism, the red "Liberty cap" became a symbol of the American patriots fighting for independence from England. During the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), the [Jacobins](/wiki/Jacobins "Jacobins") also adapted the red [Phrygian cap](/wiki/Phrygian_cap "Phrygian cap"), and forced the deposed King [Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") to wear one after his arrest.[[63]](#cite_note-auto-63)

### Socialism and communism

In the 19th century, with the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") and the rise of worker's movements, red became the color of [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") (especially the [Marxist](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") [variant](/wiki/Socialism_(Marxism) "Socialism (Marxism)")), and, with the [Paris Commune](/wiki/Paris_Commune "Paris Commune") of 1871, of revolution.[[64]](#cite_note-marh-64)

In the 20th century, red was the color first of the Russian [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") and then, after the success of the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") of 1917, of [communist parties](/wiki/Communist_parties "Communist parties") around the world. However, after the fall of the Soviet Union in 1991, Russia went back to the pre-revolutionary blue, white and red flag.

Red also became the color of many [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") parties in Europe, including the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") in Britain (founded 1900); the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (whose roots went back to 1863) and the [French Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(France) "Socialist Party (France)"), which dated back under different names, to 1879. The [Socialist Party of America](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") (1901–1972) and the [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA") (1919) both also chose red as their color.

Members of the [Christian-Social People's Party](/wiki/Christian-Social_People%27s_Party_(Liechtenstein) "Christian-Social People's Party (Liechtenstein)") in [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein") (founded 1918) advocated an expansion of democracy and progressive social policies, and were often referred to disparagingly as "Reds" for their social liberal leanings and party colors.[[103]](#cite_note-NA-103)

The [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"), founded in 1920, adopted the red flag and hammer and sickle emblem of the Soviet Union, which became the national symbols when the Party took power in China in 1949. Under Party leader [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong"), the Party anthem became "[The East Is Red](/wiki/The_East_Is_Red_(song) "The East Is Red (song)")",[[104]](#cite_note-104) and Mao Zedong himself was sometimes referred to as a "red sun".[[105]](#cite_note-105) During the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") in China, Party ideology was enforced by the [Red Guards](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards"), and the [sayings of Mao Zedong](/wiki/Quotations_from_Chairman_Mao_Tse-tung "Quotations from Chairman Mao Tse-tung") were published as a little red book in hundreds of millions of copies. Today the Chinese Communist Party claims to be the largest political party in the world, with eighty million members.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

Beginning in the 1960s and the 1970s, paramilitary extremist groups such as the [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction") in Germany, the [Japanese Red Army](/wiki/Japanese_Red_Army "Japanese Red Army") and the [Shining Path](/wiki/Shining_Path "Shining Path") Maoist movement in [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") used red as their color. But in the 1980s, some European socialist and social democratic parties, such as the Labour Party in Britain and the Socialist Party in France, moved away from the symbolism of the far left, keeping the red color but changing their symbol to a less-threatening red rose.

Red is used around the world by political parties of the left or center-left. In the United States, it is the color of the Communist Party USA, and of the [Social Democrats, USA](/wiki/Social_Democrats,_USA "Social Democrats, USA").

### United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Red_states_and_blue_states_of_the_US_based_on_data_from_the_2008%2C_2012%2C_2016_and_2020_presidential_elections.svg/250px-Red_states_and_blue_states_of_the_US_based_on_data_from_the_2008%2C_2012%2C_2016_and_2020_presidential_elections.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Red_states_and_blue_states_of_the_US_based_on_data_from_the_2008,_2012,_2016_and_2020_presidential_elections.svg)

A map of the U.S. showing the [blue states](/wiki/Blue_states "Blue states"), which voted for the Democratic candidate in the 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020 presidential elections, and the [red states](/wiki/Red_states "Red states"), which voted for the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")

In the United States, political commentators often refer to the "red states", which voted for Republican candidates in the last four presidential elections, and "blue states", which voted for Democrats. This convention is relatively recent: before the [2000 presidential election](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election "2000 United States presidential election"), media outlets assigned red and blue to both parties, sometimes alternating the allocation for each election. Fixed usage was established during the 39-day recount following the 2000 election, when the media began to discuss the contest in terms of ["red states" versus "blue states"](/wiki/Red_states_and_blue_states "Red states and blue states").[[107]](#cite_note-107) States which voted for different parties in two of the last four presidential elections are called "Swing States", and are usually colored purple, a mix of red and blue.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

## Social and special interest groups

Such names as Red Club (a bar), Red Carpet (a discothèque) or Red Cottbus and Club Red (event locations) suggest liveliness and excitement. The [Red Hat Society](/wiki/Red_Hat_Society "Red Hat Society") is a social group founded in 1998 for women 50 and over. Use of the color red to call attention to an emergency situation is evident in the names of such organizations as the Red Cross (humanitarian aid), Red Hot Organization (AIDS support), and the Red List of Threatened Species (of [IUCN](/wiki/IUCN "IUCN")). In reference to humans, term "red" is often used in the West to describe the [indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas").[[109]](#cite_note-109)

## Idioms

Many idiomatic expressions exploit the various connotations of red:
**Expressing emotion**

* "to see red" (to be angry or aggressive)[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)
* "to have red ears / a red face" (to be embarrassed)[[112]](#cite_note-112)
* "to paint the town red" (to have an enjoyable evening, usually with a generous amount of eating, drinking, dancing)[[113]](#cite_note-113)

**Giving warning**

* "to raise a red flag" (to signal that something is problematic)[[114]](#cite_note-114)
* "like a red rag to a bull" (to cause someone to be enraged)[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)
* "to be in the red" (to be losing money, from the accounting convention of writing deficits and losses in [red ink](/wiki/Red_ink "Red ink"))[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)

**Calling attention**

* "a [red letter day](/wiki/Red_letter_day "Red letter day")" (a special or important event, from the medieval custom of printing the dates of saints' days and holy days in red ink.)[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)
* "to roll out the [red carpet](/wiki/Red_carpet "Red carpet")" (to formally welcome an important guest)[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-:4-122)
* "to give red-carpet treatment" (to treat someone as important or special)[[122]](#cite_note-:4-122)
* "to catch someone red-handed" (to catch or discover someone doing something bad or wrong)[[123]](#cite_note-123)

**Other idioms**

* "to tie up in [red tape](/wiki/Red_tape "Red tape")". In England red tape was used by lawyers and government officials to identify important documents. It became a term for excessive bureaucratic regulation. It was popularized in the 19th century by the writer [Thomas Carlyle](/wiki/Thomas_Carlyle "Thomas Carlyle"), who complained about "red-tapism".[[124]](#cite_note-124)
* "[red herring](/wiki/Red_herring "Red herring")". A false clue that leads investigators off the track. Refers to the practice of using a fragrant smoked fish to distract hunting or tracking dogs from the track they are meant to follow.[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)
* "red ink" (to show a business loss)[[127]](#cite_note-127)
